1:
The chapter opens with a muster of the whole realm, and the witnesses first take the measure of who exactly stood before the dying king. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the summons as a national assembly like the one David had called before removing the ark in 1 Chron. 13:1 and 15:3, and he catalogues the ranks the verse names: the princes of the tribes listed in 1 Chron. 27:16, the captains or princes of the monthly courses of 27:1, the stewards of the royal estates in 27:25, the officers (literally eunuchs, but here simply courtiers, as in Gen. 37:36), and the mighty men or heroes of 1 Chron. 11:31. He notes pointedly that every dignitary of the previous chapter is present except the priests and Levites, an omission the closing verse will quietly repair.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch stand back to see the whole design: David, before his death, gathered the estates of the kingdom to secure Solomon's succession and to launch the great work of the reign, the building of the temple. In one solemn address he would designate his God-chosen heir, charge him to keep the commandments and serve the Lord with a devoted heart, hand over the plans of the sacred house, and call the assembly to a freewill offering for it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the gathering into a portrait of a servant finishing his course. David had served his generation by the will of God, as in Acts 13:36, and now, the nearer he came to his end, the more busy he grew, a type, Henry says, of the Son of David, who did his work with all his might. As Moses had summoned Israel to take his leave in Deut. 31:28 and Joshua in Josh. 23:2, so David would not settle the crown except in the presence of the representatives of his people.

The wesleyan-arminian voices dwell on the same energy in a failing man. John Wesley marks that David, lately recovered from the weakness of 1 Kings 1:1, improved his recovery as an opportunity to do God and his country a little more service; Adam Clarke reads the verse simply as the convening of those whose names and offices the previous chapter had already recorded. Even the reformed John Gill, brief here, fixes the scene by identifying the assembled princes as the heads of the tribes. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the stage, a king at the end of his strength calling the nation together to give his throne, his God, and his temple into the hands of his son.
2:
David rises to speak, and the witnesses gather around the single gesture the verse records, an old and ailing king getting to his feet before his people. The reformed John Gill supposes he rose from the bed or couch on which he had lain, recovered enough of his palsy to exert himself, and recalls the ancient decorum by which even kings stood to address a multitude, citing how the wounded Agamemnon would not speak sitting without first excusing it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sharpens the pathos: David was now very old and mostly confined to his bed, as 1 Kings 1:47 shows, and while he had charged Solomon lying down, for the assembled nobles of his kingdom he received strength to arise and stand upon his feet.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch qualify that picture. The king rose to speak standing, they agree, but till then he had been sitting, not lying in bed; they will not draw from 1 Kings 1:1, as Kimchi did, that he was bedridden. Their deeper interest is the address that follows, "As for me, I had in mine heart to build an house of rest for the ark and for the footstool of our God," words, they note, that David had already spoken in substance to Solomon in 1 Chron. 22:7.

Here the witnesses divide over two small words, "my brethren." Keil and Delitzsch, with Gill, hear in "Hear me, my brethren, and my people" the language of condescending goodwill, a king stooping in affection to his subjects. Charles Ellicott resists this: the phrase implies not so much condescension as an acknowledgment of what every hearer felt to be true, that all Israel were kin and David the head of the family. John Wesley grounds the same word in the law that a king's heart be not lifted above his brethren, Deut. 17:20, so that the address is a humility owed and not a graciousness bestowed; and Matthew Henry observes that David, who had once been pleased to reckon them all his servants as in 1 Chron. 21:3, now calls them his brethren whom he loved.

On the temple itself the voices converge. Ellicott explains the "house of rest" as a permanent abode in place of a wandering tent, the settled dwelling the pilgrim longing of Ps. 132:8 had hoped for, and the "footstool of our God" as the golden mercy-seat over the ark, the very throne of Deity in Ps. 99:1. Wesley draws the lesson out: heaven is God's throne, and the earth with its most magnificent temples is but his footstool, so much difference lies between the manifestations of his glory in the upper and the lower world. The witnesses leave the verse having shown a humbled king, standing in reverence before both God and the assembly, to disclose a purpose he was never permitted to fulfil.
3:
The purpose disclosed, the verse records its refusal: "But God said unto me, Thou shalt not build an house for my name." The reformed John Gill notes that this word came by the mouth of Nathan the prophet, the same messenger who had first carried both the promise and the prohibition to David. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fastens on the grammar of the contrast: the emphasis falls hard on the opening word, "God said," set in deliberate opposition to the "I, in mine heart it was" of the previous verse. Man proposes the house, but God disposes who shall raise it, a summary, Ellicott adds, of the fuller scene in 1 Chron. 22:8.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical witness, gather the reason the verse gives, "because thou hast been a man of war, and hast shed blood," and read it not as a stain of guilt but as a disqualification of office. David had prepared labour and materials for the building, they observe, as 1 Chron. 22:2 and 22:14 record; the purpose was sincere and the preparation real. Yet the house of rest for the footstool of God must be built by a man of peace, not by the warrior whose hands, however righteous their wars, had been filled with blood. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke simply refers the prohibition back to its first announcement in 2 Sam. 7:6 and 7:13, where the LORD had reserved the temple for David's seed.

The witnesses do not contend, and the theology they draw is quietly weighty. The bar is not moral punishment, for nowhere is David charged with sin in his wars, but a fitness in the type: the temple is a house of rest, and rest is not the warrior's gift to give. There is a division of labour in the kingdom of God, as the surrounding chapters make plain. David must serve the public with the sword and subdue the enemies round about, that Solomon, the man of peace, may serve it with the line and plummet. The disappointment is real, for the king had set his heart on the work and would not live to see it, yet he receives the refusal without murmur and turns at once to prepare, so far as he may, for the son who will do what he cannot. So the verse holds together the sincerity of David's devotion and the sovereignty of God's appointment, and teaches that a good desire is not always a granted commission, and that God may honour a man by the very work he assigns to another.
4:
From the refusal David turns to the election that lies behind his throne: "the LORD God of Israel chose me before all the house of my father." The reformed John Gill marks the sovereignty of it. God passed by all David's elder brethren and pitched upon the youngest, ordering him to be anointed and made king, "for ever," which Gill takes of a long reign, of his enduring posterity, and best of all of his antitype, great David's greater Son. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays out the descending choice as a chain of divine preferences that override every human precedence. Judah was not Jacob's eldest, yet God made it the ruling tribe and entailed the sceptre on it in Gen. 49:10; the house of Jesse was not the senior house even of Judah; and David was the youngest son of Jesse. It went, Henry says, by worth and not by age, for God takes whom he likes and likes whom he makes like himself.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the reading and enriches its language. "He hath chosen Judah to be the ruler" is better rendered "For Judah it was that he chose for prince," the leader or nagid of 1 Chron. 5:2; and the clause "among the sons of my father he liked me" is, he says, scarcely adequate to the Hebrew verb, which answers to the Greek word for being well pleased. Translate rather, Ellicott urges, "It was I in whom he took pleasure." And then he makes the connection that gives the verse its depth: David uses of himself the very phrase which the divine voice would one day speak from heaven over the Son of David at his baptism in Matt. 3:17. The elected shepherd-king is a shadow of the beloved Son in whom the Father is well pleased.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the "for ever" with an eye to the same horizon: the government should have had no end had Israel continued to walk in God's commandments, a condition the next verses will press, but as it refers to Christ the kingdom is, and will be, without end. The witnesses converge on a single confession. David's crown rested on no right of birth and no strength of his own, but on the free good pleasure of God, who chose the tribe, the family, and the man, and who, in choosing them, was already sketching the lineaments of the Christ to come.
5:
The election narrows from David to his heir: "of all my sons he hath chosen Solomon my son to sit upon the throne of the kingdom of the LORD." The reformed John Gill notes only that David had many sons, whose names and birth-order the earlier genealogy had given, and that from among them all God's choice fell on Solomon. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry adds the reason of that choice as he reads it: Solomon was one of the youngest of David's sons, yet God chose him to sit upon the throne because he was the likeliest of them all to build the temple, the wisest and best inclined; so it seemed good in the divine sight.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the arresting phrase that the verse alone in all the Old Testament employs. David had many sons indeed, for the genealogy of 1 Chron. 3:1 names nineteen besides the sons of the concubines, but the throne is called neither Solomon's nor David's but "the throne of the kingdom of the LORD over Israel," an expression unique in Scripture, which he compares with 1 Chron. 29:23 and 17:14. It throws into strong relief, Ellicott says, the idea that the Israelite monarchy was only a vicegerency: not David, not Solomon, but Jehovah was the true and only King. The king on the throne is a steward of a crown not his own, a truth already sounded when Gideon refused the offered kingship in Judg. 8:23, and when the LORD told Samuel that in asking a king Israel had rejected not the prophet but himself, in 1 Sam. 8:7.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers a double weight. On the one hand it magnifies grace: as God had chosen the youngest son of Jesse, so now he chooses one of the youngest sons of David, and the succession is settled by heaven's pleasure and not by the law of the firstborn. On the other it humbles the office: the throne Solomon is to mount belongs to God, who lends it to whom he will and reclaims it when he pleases. Henry's note and Ellicott's meet here, for the man is chosen for a work, the building of the house, and the throne he is given is a seat under a higher throne. Solomon, wisest of David's sons and type of the Wisdom to come, is set upon the kingdom of the LORD to build the LORD a house; the honour and the accountability are alike God's gift.
6:
Now David repeats the oracle he had received concerning his son: "Solomon thy son, he shall build my house and my courts; for I have chosen him to be my son, and I will be his father." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the force of the Hebrew. "He shall build" is emphatic, he it is that shall build, he and not thou, the very exclusion the previous verses had pronounced over David now turned into a commission for Solomon; and the adoption formula is rendered with equal stress, "I have chosen him for myself as a son, and I, I will become to him a father."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in that formula the accent of prophecy. When God says "I have chosen him to be my son," he declares the decree of Ps. 2:7, "Thou art my Son"; Solomon was the son of God's love, named Jedidiah, beloved of the LORD, and in that very love a figure of the beloved Son who was to come. And when God says "he shall build my house," Henry looks past the stone temple to Christ, who is both the founder and the foundation of the gospel temple, while the promise to establish his kingdom for ever must find its true accomplishment in the kingdom of the Messiah, which continues through all the ages of time, as Isa. 9:7 and Luke 1:33 foretell. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the verse the same way in a single stroke: in being appointed to build God's house, Solomon was a figure of him that was to come, who is both the founder and the foundation of the gospel-temple.

So the witnesses lift the verse deliberately above its first horizon. Solomon is genuinely the son and temple-builder of the oracle, but every term the oracle uses, chosen son, beloved, builder of the house, king of an everlasting kingdom, strains beyond him toward the greater Son whom he shadows. Henry and Wesley, the puritan and the Wesleyan, meet in the same typology without a hair's breadth between them, and Ellicott's grammar serves it: the emphatic "he it is that shall build" both settles the succession on Solomon and leaves room for the One of whom Solomon is the type, who builds the house not made with hands. The adoption of the son thus becomes, in the witnesses' reading, a window onto the eternal Sonship, and the building of the courts a parable of the church.
7:
The oracle carries a condition, and the witnesses weigh it: "I will establish his kingdom for ever, if he be constant to do my commandments and my judgments, as at this day." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the parallels side by side. The same promise with the same proviso is made to Solomon in 1 Kings 9:4, and the kindred pledge of long life hangs on the like condition in 1 Kings 3:14; and he reads "as at this day" as meaning "as we are doing in our present work," the same phrase that seals Solomon's own prayer in 1 Kings 8:61. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry underscores the "if": Solomon was now towardly and good, and if he continued so his kingdom would continue, but otherwise not; perseverance, he says, wears the crown, though it wins it not. The favour of God, in Henry's reading, is kept by the same constancy that first received the promise.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley meets the difficulty this raises head-on. How can an everlasting kingdom be made to hang on a condition? By distinguishing its two references: this promise, he says, is absolute with regard to the Messiah, but conditional with regard to Solomon. The throne of David's house shall stand for ever unconditionally in Christ, but for Solomon personally the establishment turns on his obedience, for if we are constant in our duty, then, and not otherwise, may we expect the continuance of God's favour. The condition does not imperil the covenant; it only measures the individual king's share in it.

The witnesses do not finally divide, but their emphases interlock in an instructive tension. Ellicott's cross-references show that the conditional form is no accident of this verse but the settled shape of God's word to Solomon throughout his reign; Henry draws from it the pastoral warning that to have begun well is not enough without continuing well; Wesley guards the whole from seeming to make the Messianic promise itself contingent. Together they hold two truths that the later history would tragically illustrate: that God's covenant with David's line is sure and everlasting in its ultimate reference, and that Solomon the man could and did forfeit his own portion of it by the very inconstancy this verse warned against. The "for ever" is safe in Christ; the "if" is aimed at Solomon, and through him at every heir of promise who must be constant to keep what he has freely received.
8:
David turns from the son to the whole assembly and lays them under a solemn charge: "Now therefore in the sight of all Israel, and in the audience of our God, keep and seek for all the commandments of the LORD your God." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the doubled witness the cadence of Moses: as Moses called heaven and earth to witness in Deut. 4:26 and 30:19, so David adjures the nation before its own eyes and before the ever-open ear of God. "Seek," he explains, means do not neglect, but resort to the commandments always as the rule of right living, the same verb used of seeking the LORD in 1 Chron. 15:13; and the motive, "that ye may possess this good land and leave it for an inheritance," is a deliberate echo of Deut. 4:1 and the law of inheritance in Lev. 25:46.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley parses the two verbs into a whole discipline of obedience: keep the commandments you already know, and seek, that is search into, those you do not yet understand, that you may know the whole will of God and seriously give yourself to the practice of it; for God's commandments cannot be kept without great care. Adam Clarke, the other wesleyan-arminian voice, lingers on the phrase "in the audience of our God," which the Targum renders "before the Word of the LORD," the divine Witness present to the whole transaction.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the solemnity into a courtroom scene. David charges them in the sight of all Israel and in the hearing of God, so that both God and the congregation stand as witnesses that fair counsel and fair warning have been given; if the people will not take it, the fault is their own, and God and man will be witnesses against them, a weight Henry compares to the charges Paul lays before God and the elect angels in 1 Tim. 5:21 and 2 Tim. 4:1. The reformed John Gill adds only that the assembly present was the congregation of the LORD, representing all Israel, so that the charge reached beyond the room to the whole covenant people. The witnesses agree that this is no mere exhortation but an adjuration: a dying king, before the double tribunal of the nation and its God, binds the people to the law on which their tenure of the land depends, obedience the condition, the good land the inheritance, and heaven itself the witness to the terms.
9:
Then the king turns full upon his son with the charge that is the heart of the chapter: "And thou, Solomon my son, know thou the God of thy father, and serve him with a perfect heart and with a willing mind." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs each term. "Know" means regard, have care for, as in Ps. 1:6; the "God of thy father" he takes, from David's own "my God" in v.20, to mean the God of David personally, not merely the God of Israel; a "perfect heart" is a whole, sound, and undivided heart, the integer allegiance the Decalogue demands; and a "willing mind" is required because service is not real unless it be voluntary, glad as well as free.

On the ground of the charge the witnesses agree: "the LORD searcheth all hearts." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley makes it the death of hypocrisy, for put on a mere profession to please your father, and you may deceive me, but him you cannot deceive, since he searches the very motions of the heart. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry joins knowledge and service inseparably: we cannot serve God aright if we do not know him, and in vain do we know him if we do not serve him; the perfect heart is sincerity, which is our gospel perfection, grounded in the truth that God understands every imagination of the thoughts, so that even the heart of kings, unsearchable to men as Prov. 25:3 says, lies open to him.

The seam opens over the promise and the threat: "if thou seek him, he will be found of thee; but if thou forsake him, he will cast thee off for ever." Henry reads it as strictly reciprocal, for seek him diligently and he will be found, as Heb. 11:6 assures, but forsake him and he will cast you off; and yet, Henry insists, God never casts any off till they have first cast him off. Wesley presses the sobering force of the threat even against the dynastic promise, for the casting-off would stand notwithstanding all God's promises to David and to his seed. But Ellicott will not let the condition harden into a rule that binds God: seeking in earnest always finds, he grants, as Isa. 55:6 teaches, yet the divine grace is not restricted even by this condition, for God is found of them that sought him not in Isa. 65:1. So the debate is joined: is finding God the strict reward of seeking, forfeited only by our own forsaking, or may grace outrun and anticipate the seeker? The word rendered "cast off," Ellicott notes, is a strong one, to reject as a thing foul-smelling, as in Hos. 8:3. The witnesses leave the charge poised between a real condition and a grace not bounded by it.
10:
The charge sharpens to its point: "Take heed now; for the LORD hath chosen thee to build an house for the sanctuary: be strong, and do it." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott would render the opening "See now that Jehovah hath chosen thee," and paraphrases it as a summons to consider this high commission, to weigh it well and realise it thoroughly, and then to be strong and act; it is the same charge David had given in 1 Chron. 22:13, now made urgent because the hour of action has come. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "be strong" as a call to take courage to break through all difficulties, for without this we can do no work of God as we ought.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the two imperatives of the verse in balance, a caution and a courage. "Take heed" is the caution: beware of everything that looks like, or leads to, that which is evil. "Be strong, and do it" is the courage: we cannot do our work as we should unless we put on resolution and fetch in strength from divine grace. And Henry ties the building to the holiness just enjoined, for because Solomon is to build a house for the sanctuary, therefore let him seek and serve God, that the work may be done from a good principle, in a right manner, and be accepted. The reformed John Gill reads "take heed" first as a warning against the very sin the previous verse had named, take heed of offending God by forsaking him, and then as an alerting of the mind to what David is about to hand over.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers the two halves of David's charge into one. The inward call, to know and serve God with a perfect heart, and the outward call, to build the house for the sanctuary, are not two tasks but one; the temple must be raised by a man who himself seeks the God whose house it is. Ellicott's "realise it thoroughly," Wesley's courage against difficulty, and Henry's pairing of caution with resolution all press the same nerve: that the greatest work in Israel demanded not merely skill and treasure but a chosen and consecrated builder, one who took heed to his own soul as carefully as to the plans about to be delivered into his hands. The transition is exact, for the next verse will turn from the man to the model, from "be strong and do it" to the pattern he is to do.
11:
David now places the plans themselves into Solomon's hands: "the pattern of the porch, and of the houses thereof, and of the treasuries, and of the place of the mercy seat." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott follows the description from the outside in. The word "pattern" is the same term used of the model of the tabernacle shown to Moses in Exod. 25:9; the "porch" is the vestibule of 1 Kings 6:3; the "houses" are the temple's two principal rooms, the holy place and the holy of holies; the "treasuries," a word occurring here only, a Persian loan-word for store-chambers, were probably the three-storeyed side-building of 1 Kings 6:5; the "upper chambers" lay above the holiest; and the whole ends at "the place of the mercy seat," the inmost shrine.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sees in the delivery something like an architect's portfolio: David gave Solomon an ichnograph of the building, with elevations, sections, and specifications of every part, and all of it, Clarke insists, received by inspiration from God himself, just as Moses had received the plan of the tabernacle. The reformed John Gill and John Wesley fill in the several rooms, the porch as the eastward entrance, the houses as the holy and most holy places, and the parlours or side-chambers built against the wall as in 1 Kings 6:5.

But it is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who draws out the theology of the pattern, and he does so by insisting that nothing here was left to human contrivance. As Moses had a pattern shown him in the mount in Heb. 8:5, so David had this by the immediate hand of God upon him; the temple was a sacred thing and a type of Christ, a kind of sacrament, and therefore must not be framed by man's art or invention but by divine institution. The contrivance of David's devotion or Solomon's wisdom, Henry warns, must not be trusted in a matter of this nature; Christ the true temple, the church the gospel temple, and heaven the everlasting temple are all framed according to the divine counsels laid before the world began. When Christ later left his disciples the model of his gospel church, Henry adds, he charged them to observe that only which he commanded. So the witnesses converge on a single conviction that the next verses will make explicit: the house of God is God's own design, delivered to the builder but originating in heaven, and Solomon's task is not to invent but faithfully to execute a given plan.
12:
The source of the plan now becomes the question, for the verse says David had the pattern "by the spirit," and here the witnesses genuinely divide. The reformed John Gill reads it of the Spirit of God, who gave David the whole form of the house, just as God had shown Moses the pattern of the tabernacle in the mount; the Targum, he notes, renders it "by the spirit of prophecy," and some of the Jews supposed the design was first made known to Samuel the prophet, who conveyed it to David. Adam Clarke, wesleyan-arminian, agrees flatly with the Targum, "by the spirit of prophecy that was with him," and John Wesley, of the same school, reasons that since all the particulars of the Mosaic tabernacle were suggested by God's Spirit, it is not credible that God would use less care and exactness in this far more glorious and durable work.

The grammatical-historical witnesses hear the phrase differently. Keil and Delitzsch take "the pattern of all that he had by the spirit" to mean the description of all that lay in David's own spirit, what his spirit had designed, the courts of the house, the chambers round about, and the treasuries of 1 Chron. 26:20. Charles Ellicott presses the same reading: the words mean "the pattern of all that had come to be in the spirit with him," that is, had come into his mind, the whole design as it lay there, exactly parallel to the "with my heart it was to build" of the earlier verse. On this reading the "spirit" is David's, not immediately the Holy Spirit, though Ellicott is careful to note that the following verse attributes the design's ultimate origin to divine inspiration.

So the debate is real, and it turns on a single ambiguity: does "by the spirit" name the Spirit of God revealing a heavenly blueprint, as at Sinai, or David's own inspired mind conceiving the plan under God's guidance? Gill, Clarke, and Wesley take the former, pressing the analogy to Moses and the mount; Keil and Ellicott take the latter, reading the Hebrew as the psychology of design rather than the mode of revelation. Yet the division is not as wide as it looks, for even the grammatical-historical witnesses do not empty the plan of divine authorship, and Gill himself will allow that David's mind was the tablet on which the Spirit wrote. The witnesses leave the verse having exposed the seam that the great claim of v.19 will draw tight: whether the pattern came down to David or arose within him, all agree it came, in the end, from God.
13:
The catalogue continues with the living service the house was built to hold: "also for the courses of the priests and the Levites, and for all the work of the service, and for all the vessels of service." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott connects the clause tightly to the chambers just named: the cells round the temple court were meant not only for storing the treasures but for the priests and Levites who would sojourn in them by their courses. He grants that the Septuagint and Vulgate render the words as though David gave a description of the courses themselves, a sense the Hebrew allows, but he prefers the closer connection with the chambers. "The work of the service," he explains, was the homely labour of the sanctuary, the cooking of the flesh that fell to the priests from the sacrifices and the baking of the shewbread; and the "vessels of service" were the utensils kept in those cells for such work. He cites the old Syriac paraphrase, which spells the buildings out, the colonnade, the upper chambers, the inner and outer cloisters, the treasury, the kitchens, the house of the water-carriers, and the house of the lampmen.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, read the verse as one continuous construction with what precedes: for the divisions of the priests and Levites, for all the work of the service, and for all the vessels; the cells and the buildings of the courts were set apart for all these purposes, the sojourn of the ministers in their turns, the preparation of the shewbread and the cooking of the sacrificial flesh, and the storing of the vessels those tasks required. With the following verse, they note, the enumeration turns from the buildings to the vessels themselves. The reformed John Gill adds simply that these were the courses David had lately fixed, appointing where each should serve.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse quietly enlarges the meaning of the pattern. The plan handed to Solomon is not merely architectural but liturgical: it provides not only walls and chambers but the ordered ministry that will fill them, the priests and Levites in their rotations, the daily labour of altar and table, the very kitchens and store-rooms where the work is done. The house of God, in the witnesses' reading, was designed down to its service; the God who gave the shape of the porch and the place of the mercy-seat gave also the arrangement of those who would minister within, so that nothing in his worship should be left to improvisation. What the earlier verses called a sanctuary this verse reveals as a working house, whose every function was foreseen and provided for in the pattern from above.
14:
With the buildings described, the verse turns to the weighing out of the metals: "he gave of gold by weight for things of gold, silver also for all instruments of silver by weight." The reformed John Gill takes the repeated "by weight" as the mark of divine direction: whatever was to be made of gold, David was shown by the Spirit of God the exact weight or quantity to be used in the making of it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the concise Hebrew as continuing the construction of the pattern itself, so that David gave Solomon a schedule for the golden vessels by weight and for the silver vessels by weight, an inventory specifying the exact weight of each; he compares the careful weighing of the temple treasures at the return from exile in Ezra 8:25. Adam Clarke, wesleyan-arminian, glosses it in a line: the weight named was the quantity of gold to be put into each article.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical witness, note that the enumeration of vessels here begins, and that a word must be supplied at the head of the list from the "pattern" of the earlier verses, so that David gave the pattern of the forks and the golden tankards, each according to its weight. Together the witnesses read the verse less as a bare accountant's note than as the extension of the divine plan into its smallest particulars: the same God who fixed the shape of the house fixed the mass of every vessel within it.

The witnesses do not divide, and the theology they draw is the exactness of holy things. Nothing in the temple was to be made at the craftsman's discretion, neither its dimensions nor its weights; the gold of a lampstand and the silver of a bason were told out beforehand, so that the finished vessel might answer precisely to the pattern given. Gill's insistence that even the quantities were by the Spirit of God, and Ellicott's picture of a written schedule of weights, meet in the same point, that God's house was to be built by measure and not by guess, and that David's provision left the builder no room to make the vessels either poorer or, in a false zeal, larger than the plan appointed. The lavishness of the gold is real, but it is a governed lavishness; every shekel is weighed, because in the worship of God even generosity must be obedient, conforming itself to a revealed and definite will rather than to the impulse of the giver.
15:
The weights are now itemised, beginning with the lampstands: "the weight for the candlesticks of gold, and for their lamps of gold, and for the candlesticks of silver by weight." The reformed John Gill, drawing on Jewish authorities, records the tradition that there were ten golden candlesticks, each bearing seven lamps, set five on the one side of the old Mosaic candlestick and five on the other, and that the weight of gold appointed for each was a hundred pounds. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the same increase more soberly: there had been but one chandelier in the tabernacle, but there were ten in the temple, as 1 Kings 7:49 records.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the verse as a schedule of designated weights, so much for the golden lampstands and their lamps, weighed for each stand and its lamps, and so much for the silver lampstands according to the service of each, and refers the great golden candelabrum of the sanctuary back to Exod. 25:31. But he pauses on a curiosity: the silver lampstands mentioned here are named nowhere else in the Old Testament, and, according to the Rabbis, they stood not in the holy place but in the chambers of the priests. John Wesley, of the same wesleyan-arminian school as Clarke, reads the closing phrase "according to the use of every candlestick" as distinguishing those that were fixed in one place, the ten in the holy place, from any that were to be carried from place to place.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse becomes a small parable of the temple's greater glory. Where the tabernacle had one lampstand, the temple has ten; where the wilderness sanctuary was a single moving light, the settled house blazes with a multiplied radiance. Gill's hundred pounds of gold to a stand, Clarke's arithmetic of one grown to ten, and Ellicott's schedule of exact weights all press the same impression, that the house of rest was to be furnished on a scale the pilgrim tent could never bear. Yet the multiplication is not extravagance for its own sake but proportion: a larger and more permanent house required a fuller light, and the golden lamps, weighed out to the pound, were to fill the holy place with a brightness answerable to the settled dwelling of God among his people. Even the light, in David's pattern, was told out by weight.
16:
Next come the tables: "by weight he gave gold for the tables of shewbread, for every table; and likewise silver for the tables of silver." The reformed John Gill notes the same multiplication that marked the lampstands, for where the Mosaic tabernacle had but one table of shewbread, the temple of Solomon had ten, each of a hundred pounds' weight in gold. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms that the Law spoke of only one table, as Exod. 25:23 and 1 Kings 7:48 show, and that the chronicler was well aware of it; the ten golden tables set five on the right and five on the left, recorded in 2 Chron. 4:8, must be the tables meant here.

But Ellicott ventures a textual conjecture that stands somewhat apart from the other witnesses. It may be, he suggests, that the word rendered "shewbread" is a gloss which has displaced the word "gold," and that the original text read simply "for the tables of gold," balancing "for the tables of silver" at the verse's end, in which case the single table of shewbread would be included among the golden tables rather than named on its own. The silver tables, he adds, are spoken of nowhere else in the Old Testament; the Rabbis assert that they stood in the court, and that the prepared flesh of the sacrifices was laid upon them. John Wesley, wesleyan-arminian, resolves the plural more simply: there were divers tables used about the shewbread, one of them more eminent than the rest, and this chief one is commonly called, in the singular, the table of the shewbread.

The witnesses converge on the fact and differ only on its wording. All agree that the one table of the wilderness had become many in the settled house, and that the pattern told out the gold and silver for each by weight. Where Gill and Wesley read the plural at face value, many tables with one pre-eminent, Ellicott presses a suspicion that a copyist's gloss has clouded the verse, and would recover behind it a cleaner contrast of golden tables and silver. It is a minor divergence, and Ellicott offers it tentatively; but it shows the grammatical-historical witness doing what he does best, listening for the seams in the received text even amid a plain inventory. Whether ten tables of shewbread or golden tables simply, the point the witnesses share holds: the bread of the Presence, like the light of the lamps, was to be multiplied and its furniture weighed out, that the enlarged house might set forth the fuller provision of its God.
17:
The inventory passes to the smaller vessels of pure gold: "also pure gold for the fleshhooks, and the bowls, and the cups: and for the golden basons he gave gold by weight." The reformed John Gill explains the first of them plainly, for the fleshhooks were the forks with which the pieces of flesh were taken out of the pots in which they were boiled, and these were to be of pure gold. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the rest and their uses: the forks, the bowls, and the flagons were pure gold, as the sanctuary furniture of Exod. 27:3 had been of bronze; the bowls were used in the lustral sprinkling of blood, and the golden flagons in the pouring of libations, as Exod. 25:29 and Num. 4:7 prescribe. The "golden basons," he adds, are better rendered tankards or lidded pitchers, a word found only here and among the sacred vessels Cyrus restored in Ezra 1:10; and all of them, again, were weighed out for their several offices.

The witnesses do not divide over these vessels, and their very smallness carries a lesson the witnesses let stand. The pattern from above did not stop at the great structures, the porch, the houses, the place of the mercy-seat, nor even at the conspicuous furniture of lampstand and table; it descended to the flesh-hooks by which meat was lifted from a boiling pot, and told out the gold of them by weight no less than the gold of the sanctuary itself. Gill's homely note on the forks and Ellicott's catalogue of bowls and flagons and lidded pitchers meet in the same impression: that in the worship of God nothing was too common to be consecrated, and nothing too small to be numbered. The instruments of the kitchen and the vessels of the altar alike had their appointed weight of precious metal, because the whole service, in its least utensil, belonged to the pattern and to the God who gave it. What the earlier verse announced as a principle, that all was by weight, this verse carries down to the humblest tool, so that even the fork that served the sacrifice was pure gold, weighed and set within the plan. The great house and the little hook were governed by one and the same divine exactness, and the reverence due to God reached, in David's provision, all the way to the bottom of the inventory.
18:
The vessels culminate in two objects of special weight: "for the altar of incense refined gold by weight; and gold for the pattern of the chariot of the cherubims, that spread out their wings, and covered the ark." The reformed John Gill notes the altar of incense was to be overlaid with refined gold, a hundred pounds of it by weight; and John Wesley, wesleyan-arminian, fastens on that word "refined," purer than any of the rest, because the golden altar of incense was typical of the intercession of Christ, than which nothing can be more pure and perfect.

Then comes the strange and luminous phrase, "the pattern of the chariot of the cherubims." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that the cherubim, not the ark, are the chariot upon which God enters and is enthroned, the conception drawn from the LORD who rode upon a cherub in Ps. 18:10 and sitteth between the cherubim in Ps. 99:1; and they recall that Ezekiel saw wheels beneath that living throne. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, agrees that the two cherubs outspreading their wings over the mercy-seat are here called "the chariot," with reference to those same psalms and to the overshadowing wings commanded in Exod. 25:20. Matthew Henry, puritan-evangelical, understands these to be the two greater cherubim Solomon would make, whose wings reached from wall to wall as 1 Kings 6:23 tells, distinct from the small pair on the mercy-seat; and he calls them a chariot because the angels are the chariots of God, as Ps. 68:17 declares.

Wesley draws from the image a solemn typology the others only imply. The cherubim are called a chariot, he says, because within them God is said to sit and dwell; and because a chariot is made to carry a person from place to place, the word hints that God was not so fixed to the temple by its building but that he would remove from them if they forsook him, the very warning the ninth verse had sounded to Solomon. The witnesses do not divide; together they let the golden vessels rise to their climax in the throne of God himself. The altar of incense breathes the intercession of Christ; the cherub-chariot bears the enthroned glory; and even here, at the pattern's holiest point, Wesley hears the covenant's "if," that the God who deigns to dwell between the cherubim remains free, and stays or departs as his people cleave to him or forsake him.
19:
All at once the narrative gives David his own voice, and with it the boldest claim of the chapter: "All this, said David, the LORD made me understand in writing by his hand upon me, even all the works of this pattern." Here the witnesses openly divide over the mode of the revelation. The reformed John Gill lays out the possibilities without at first choosing: the words may mean that by an impulse of the Spirit the whole was as clearly represented to David's mind as if he had it in writing; or, as some Jews held, that the design was first made known to Samuel or some other prophet, who delivered it to David in writing. Yet Gill sees no reason to deny that David had the pattern by an actual handwriting from the LORD himself, through the ministry of angels, even as Moses received the two tables from the hand of God.

The wesleyan-arminian voices lean the same way but keep the alternatives open. John Wesley offers both: God revealed this to some man of God who put it into writing and by him conveyed it to David; or else God did, as it were, by his own hand, the hand that wrote the Ten Commandments, write these things upon the table of David's mind. Adam Clarke prefers a vision: in some ecstasy David had seen a fully sketched-out plan, whose impression was so deep that he could describe it to his son; and Clarke cites Delaney's judgment that the very architecture of the temple was of divine origin.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is the most cautious and the most disquieting. The abrupt "said David," with no introductory formula, he calls dramatic; and rendering the Hebrew strictly, "the whole in a writing from the hand of Jehovah, to me he made clear," he compares the writing from the hand of the LORD to the tables of Exod. 31:18. But whether that writing was a communication through one of David's seers, or merely the description of the Mosaic sanctuary already given in Exod. 25:9, he confesses is not clear; the verb "he taught" wants an object; and he ends by allowing that perhaps the text is corrupt. So the debate stands unresolved among the witnesses. Was the pattern a direct writing from God's own hand, a second Sinai; or was it mediated through a prophet who wrote it down; or is the mode simply obscure, the pattern perhaps only the old Mosaic plan, the very text uncertain? All agree the design was of God; they part over how it came, and Ellicott alone leaves the question raw.
20:
The plans delivered, David returns to encourage the man who must execute them: "Be strong and of good courage, and do it: fear not, nor be dismayed: for the LORD God, even my God, will be with thee." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that this resumes the charge broken off before the plans were handed over, and that "and do it" is added now because the time for action has come; the words "fear not, neither be dismayed" are lifted from the commissioning of Joshua in Deut. 31:8 and Josh. 1:5. The single phrase "my God," he says, recalls in a breath all of David's own experience of the divine Helper; and "until thou hast finished" implies nothing about the time beyond the appointed limit, a usage he compares with the "till" of Matt. 1:25.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, hear in "even my God" David's purpose to call to his son's mind how often, in many perils, that God had preserved him; the encouragement echoes his private charge to Solomon in 1 Chron. 22:13. Adam Clarke, wesleyan-arminian, gives the Targum's paraphrase, "the Word of the LORD my God will be thy assistant."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draw the promise into a rule of experience, and they say it in almost the same breath. "The Lord God, even my God," Henry paraphrases, whom I have chosen and served, who has all along been with me and prospered me, he will be with thee, he will not fail thee nor forsake thee, the same word given to Joshua in Josh. 1:5 and to every believer in Heb. 13:5. And both witnesses close on the sober converse of the promise: God never leaves any, Wesley says, unless they first leave him; God never forsakes, Henry agrees, until men have first forsaken him. The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers the chapter's two notes into one. The presence of God is the whole ground of the builder's courage, not his own skill or strength but the God who owned the fathers and carried them through the work of their day. Yet even the promise carries the chapter's "if" within it: the assurance that God will not forsake is spoken to a son just charged not to forsake God, so that the sentence which comforts also quietly warns, and the "my God" David commends is a God to be held fast if he is to be enjoyed.
21:
The chapter closes as David points Solomon to the helpers ranged about him: "behold, the courses of the priests and the Levites, and there shall be with thee every willing skilful man, also the princes and all the people will be wholly at thy commandment." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the deictic force of the opening, for the very form of expression suggests David pointed to the priests and Levites as he spoke, the representatives of religion who would hardly be absent from an assembly of all the princes of Israel, and who may be numbered among the "princes of God" of 1 Chron. 24:5. The "willing skilful man," he explains, renders a word that strictly means one who brings a freewill offering, as in Exod. 35:5: skilled craftsmen have volunteered for the work, as 1 Chron. 22:15 had promised, and will lend their art to Solomon's endeavour.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical witness, render the "behold" with Clericus as "here you have them," the divisions of the priests and Levites set at Solomon's side for help, and note a small textual roughness in the Hebrew particle before "every willing man," which some would strike out. Adam Clarke, wesleyan-arminian, gathers the whole into a portrait of David's completeness as a father: having assigned Solomon his work and described the manner of it, he now shows him his assistants and encourages him, so that piety, good sense, prudence, and zeal for the public welfare and God's glory are all united, and Solomon has his danger, his duty, and his interest set before him in the truest light.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the closing lesson from the concord of all parties. Good men will help thee, he reads: the priests and Levites will advise, the workmen are both willing and skilful, two very good properties in those who work at the temple, and the princes and people are so far from opposing the work that they are wholly at command, every one in his place ready to further it. Then good work is likely to go on, Henry says, when all concerned are hearty in furthering it and none secretly clogs it. The reformed John Gill adds only that these were the courses David had lately fixed to take their turns in the temple when built. The witnesses do not divide, and the chapter ends where a wise dying king would wish it, not on the loneliness of the great task but on the whole nation marshalled willingly behind the young builder, ministers, craftsmen, princes, and people, each in his order, given into Solomon's hand along with the pattern and the gold.
